Written by Elara J. Thornberry
Illustrations by Marcus T. Ashwood

ocr_text_blocks.content: BooK ONE: A HERO S JOURNEY

text_blocks.content: Ironfist’s Grand Adventure
Chapter 1
The Beginning
I

ronfist stepped out of his cottage and squinted against the morning sun. The sky above Wil-lowmere was a vast canvas of the most brilliant blue he had ever seen, stretching endlesslyfrom the misty hills in the east to the dark line of the Whispering Woods in the west. Paintedacross the clouds, as if by some playful god with a brush made of wind, were the words:
“IT WAS A BEAUTIFUL DAY.”
He took a deep breath. The air smelled of honeysuckle and damp earth, and somewhere inthe distance, a meadowlark was singing its heart out from the top of old Mr. Grumbleton’sweathervane.

He was very excited about today’s adventure. He had been waiting for this moment his entirelife — ever since his grandfather, the legendary warrior Steelfist, had told him stories about theancient Kingdom beyond the mountains. Those tales of dragons and enchanted forests had filledyoung Ironfist’s dreams for as long as he could remember.

“Oh my God!”Ironfist shouted, looking at the dark cloudsrolling in from the west like a stampede of angry grey horses.The morning had been so beautiful just moments ago, but nowthe wind was picking up, sending leaves spiraling across the gar-den path. “It’s raining cats and dogs already! I haven’t even left
Ironfist’s Grand Adventure
the garden yet!”
His mother appeared at the cottage door, wiping flour from herhands onto her apron. She was a kind woman with laugh lines around her eyes and silver streaksin her dark hair.
“Take your cloak, dear,” she called out. “And don’t forget — your sister Lily is waiting for youat the crossroads. She’s been up since dawn, that girl. More excited than a squirrel in a nutfactory.”

Ironfist grabbed his leather satchel, threw his heavy wool cloak over his shoulders, and set offdown the cobblestone lane that wound through the village of Willowmere. The rain was comingdown harder now, great fat drops that splattered against the stone walls and turned the guttersinto tiny rushing rivers.

As he passed the village square, he noticed old Mrs. Pembury’s cat sitting on her windowsill,watching the world go by with an expression of supreme feline indifference. The cat opened itsmouth wide and let out a tremendous:
MEOW!
“Good morning to you too, Whiskers,” Ironfist muttered, pulling his hood tighter against therain.
Chapter 2
The Goblin in the Woods
D

eep in the Whispering Woods, where the ancient oaks grew so close together that theirbranches wove into a living canopy overhead, the shadows danced and whisperedsecrets in a language only the trees could understand. Ironfist walked along the narrowtrail with careful, deliberate steps, his boots crunching softly on the carpet of fallen leaves. Everyfew paces, he would stop and listen, his hand resting on the pommel of his grandfather’s sword.
The forest was unnaturally quiet. No birdsong. No rustling of small creatures in the undergrowth.Even the wind seemed to hold its breath.
“Something isn’t right,” Ironfist whispered to himself, scanning the treeline with narrowed eyes.
Then he heard it — a low, guttural cackle that seemed to come from everywhere and nowhereat once.

Suddenly, a Goblin leaped out from behind a twisted oak! Itwas a wretched creature, no taller than Ironfist’s waist, withmottled green skin, pointed ears that twitched like a bat’s,and yellow eyes that glowed with a malevolent inner light.It wore a patchwork vest made from what appeared to bestolen curtain fabric, and it clutched a gnarled staff carvedfrom blackthorn.
Ironfist’s Grand Adventure
“I will destroy you, foolish boy!” the Goblin snarled, baring its crooked yellow teeth. “Thisforest belongs to the Goblin King! No human walks through our territory and lives to tell thetale!”
Ironfist drew his blade. The steel sang as it cleared the scabbard, catching the faint moonlightand throwing silver reflections across the dark tree trunks.

What followed was brief and brutal. He brutally killed the beast with his sword, slashing throughthe darkness like a bolt of lightning. The creature let out a terrible shriek that echoed throughthe woods, sending a murder of crows bursting from the treetops in a flurry of black wings andpanicked cawing.

When it was over, Ironfist stood panting in the clearing, his sword dripping with the strangesilvery ichor that passed for Goblin blood. He felt sick to his stomach — not from fear, but fromthe violence of what he had been forced to do. He was only sixteen, after all. He cleaned hisblade on the mossy trunk of a fallen tree and sheathed it with trembling hands.
“I’m sorry,” he murmured to the silent forest. “I didn’t want to hurt you. But you gave me nochoice.”
The woods remained quiet, but somewhere in the distance, he thought he heard the sound ofother Goblins retreating through the underbrush.
Chapter 3
Downtown Express
T

he Whispering Woods eventually gave way to rolling farmland, and the rolling farmlandgave way to the outskirts of Ashenmere City — a bustling metropolis of cobblestonestreets, towering brick buildings, and more people than Ironfist had ever seen gath-ered in one place. He had heard stories about the city from traveling merchants who visitedWillowmere, but nothing could have prepared him for the sheer noise of it all. Street vendorshawking roasted chestnuts, carriages clattering over cobblestones, dogs barking, children laugh-ing, and somewhere a street musician playing a merry tune on a battered accordion.

He checked the crumpled map his grandfather had drawn for him years ago. The next stage ofhis journey required crossing the city to reach the Northern Mountains, and the fastest way todo that was underground.

He took the Subway downtown — the rumbling train carrying him through dark tunnels beneaththe streets. The carriage was warm and smelled of damp wool and engine oil. An old womanacross from him was knitting something enormous and shapeless from bright purple yarn, anda man in a bowler hat was reading a newspaper with the headline: “GOBLIN SIGHTINGS ONTHE RISE — MAYOR URGES CALM.”
“Let’s go!”
Ironfist’s Grand Adventure
Ironfist murmured to himself as the train lurched forward into the tunnel. Through the grimywindow, he caught one last glimpse of the street above.

Outside the station, a group of children had built a Snowman.It wore a top hat and had a bright orange carrot for a nose, withtwo lumps of coal for eyes that seemed to stare at passersbywith a cheerful, frozen grin. A tattered red scarf was wrappedaround its neck, fluttering gently in the icy breeze. The childrenlaughed and threw snowballs at each other, their cheeks red asapples, their breath forming little white clouds in the frigid air.

Ironfist smiled at the scene. It reminded him of winters back in Willowmere, when he and Lilywould build snow forts in the meadow behind their cottage and wage epic snowball battles thatlasted until their mother called them in for hot cocoa.
But he couldn’t linger. The mountains were calling.
Chapter 4
The Shadow
T

he city streets grew quiet as evening fell like a heavy velvet curtain over Ashenmere. Thegas lamps flickered to life one by one along the boulevard, casting pools of amber lightthat barely penetrated the thickening fog. Most sensible people had retreated indoorsby now — Ironfist could see their silhouettes moving behind curtained windows, warm and safein their homes. The smell of cooking — roast meat, baking bread, something with cinnamon —drifted out of the houses and made his stomach growl.

He was searching for an inn where he could spend the night before setting out for the mountainsat dawn. His map indicated there should be one on Cobbler’s Lane, just past the old fountainwith the stone mermaid.
But as he turned the corner, he saw something that made him freeze mid-step.

From the dark alley ahead, a shape emerged — neither man nor woman, neither young nor old.Just a presence. A figure wrapped in a cloak so dark it seemed to swallow the lamplight aroundit. Where a face should have been, there was only shadow, and within that shadow, two pointsof pale light that might have been eyes.
They are coming closer.
Ironfist pressed his back against the cold brick wall and held his breath. His heart was hammering
Ironfist’s Grand Adventure

so loud he was certain the creature could hear it. The figure moved with an unsettling fluidity,as if it were not walking but gliding across the cobblestones, its dark cloak trailing behind likeink bleeding through water.
“Who are you?” he whispered into the fog. His voice came out thin and reedy, nothing like thebrave warrior he fancied himself to be.
But they did not answer. They simply watched, those pale eyes unblinking, and then — like acandle flame snuffed out by a sudden breeze — the figure dissolved into the mist and was gone.
Ironfist stood there for a long time, shaking, before he found the courage to move again.
Chapter 5
The Grand Feast
T

he shadow turned out to be a friend. Well, perhaps “friend” was too strong a word —it turned out to be the old innkeeper of the Silver Stag Tavern, a stooped, mysteriousfigure named Mr. Greycloak, who had a peculiar habit of skulking about in the fog to“check on the neighborhood,” as he put it. He was eccentric, certainly, but harmless, and heinvited Ironfist inside with a toothless grin and a promise of the finest Christmas feast this sideof the Northern Mountains.
And what a feast it was.

They enjoyed a big roasted Turkey for Christmas dinner.The golden bird sat at the center of the long oak table, itscrispy skin glistening with rendered fat and rosemary, sur-rounded by mountains of roasted potatoes, boats of steam-ing gravy, bowls of cranberry sauce, and platters of roastedroot vegetables. The hall was decked with boughs of hollyand ivy, and dozens of candles cast a warm, flickering glowacross the faces of the assembled guests — farmers, merchants, traveling performers, and onerather bewildered-looking teenager from Willowmere.
Ironfist held a juicy Hamburger in his other hand — his favorite comfort food, even at a fancy
Ironfist’s Grand Adventure

feast. The innkeeper had prepared it specially for him after learning that the boy had a peculiarfondness for ground beef patties between two halves of a sesame-seed bun. It was, Ironfistreflected as he took an enormous bite, possibly the best Hamburger he had ever eaten.
BAKERY

On the wall behind the head table hung a wooden signboard that read “BAKERY” in ornategolden letters — a relic from the tavern’s previous life as the village bakehouse. Mr. Greycloakwas rather proud of it.

“My grandmother baked the finest sourdough in three counties,” he told Ironfist, gesturing at thesign with a gnarled finger. “People came from miles around. That sign hung above her door forfifty years.”

Through the tall arched windows, fireworks exploded in the starry December sky — great bloom-ing chrysanthemums of red, gold, and green that lit up the snow-covered rooftops and cast danc-ing shadows across the feast.
BOOM!

The children at the far end of the table squealed with delight. An old man spilled his soup.And Ironfist grinned from ear to ear, thinking that perhaps adventures weren’t only about fight-ing Goblins and chasing shadows — sometimes they were about sitting in a warm room withstrangers who felt like family, eating too much food, and watching the sky catch fire.
Chapter 6
The Mountain Trail
T

he next morning, Ironfist and his little sister Lily set out for the Rocky Mountains. Shehad been waiting for him at the crossroads just outside town, bouncing on her heelswith barely contained excitement, her cheeks rosy from the cold and a knitted hat pulleddown over her pigtails.

“You should be careful,” Ironfist warned his sister as they began the steep ascent up the Thorn-back Ridge. “The trail can be dangerous. One wrong step and you’ll tumble down into thevalley. And I’d rather not explain to Mother how I lost her youngest daughter.”
“You worry too much,” Lily replied cheerfully, skipping ahead over the loose stones with thefearless agility of a mountain goat. “Race you to the top!”

The old mountain guide was waiting for them at the Shepherd’sCairn — a weathered man with a beard like a snowdrift and eyesthe color of glacier ice. He’d been guiding travelers throughthese mountains for forty years, and he knew every ridge, everyravine, every treacherous patch of black ice that could send theunwary sliding a thousand feet to their doom.
He squinted at his ancient map through a pair of spectacles heldtogether with string and hope, and said in a voice like gravel rolling downhill:
Ironfist’s Grand Adventure

“Walk 15 miles to the North. Bring 3 pounds of dried food and plenty of water. It’s nearly 100degrees Fahrenheit out there in the sun, believe it or not. The altitude plays tricks. You’ll freezein the shadows and roast on the ridgeline.”
Ironfist converted the figures in his head — that was roughly 24 kilometers, about 1.4 kilogramsof food, and nearly 38 degrees Celsius. The old Imperial measurements still confused himsometimes.
“How long will the crossing take?” Lily asked, peering up at the towering peaks with wide eyes.

“Two days if the weather holds. Three if it doesn’t. A lifetime if you take a wrong turn.” Theguide chuckled darkly. “But don’t worry, little one. I haven’t lost a traveler yet. Well... notrecently, anyway.”
Lily looked worried, but Ironfist squeezed her hand.
“Don’t worry,” he said gently. “I’ll protect you. That’s what big brothers are for.”

They set off up the mountain as the sun climbed higher, painting the snow-covered peaks inshades of rose and gold. The air was thin and sharp, tasting of pine resin and glacial meltwater,and every breath felt like drinking ice.
Chapter 7
Campfire Stories
T

hey set up camp near a quiet stream that gurgled over smooth stones on its way down tothe valley far below. The stars blazed overhead like diamonds scattered across blackvelvet — more stars than Ironfist had ever seen, even on the clearest nights in Wil-lowmere. Without the glow of city lights, the Milky Way was visible as a vast, luminous riverof light flowing from one horizon to the other. It was, Ironfist thought, the most beautiful thinghe had ever seen.
As they roasted marshmallows — Lily liked hers barely toasted,Ironfist preferred his charred black on the outside and moltenin the middle — Ironfist tried to make his sister laugh with ariddle.
“Here’s one Grandfather used to tell me,” he said, turning hismarshmallow slowly over the flames. “Time flies like an arrow;fruit flies like a banana!”
Lily giggled so hard she nearly dropped her marshmallow into the fire. “That doesn’t even makesense!” she exclaimed.
“That’s the whole point,” said Ironfist, poking the fire with a long stick and sending a shower oforange sparks spiraling up into the starry sky. “Some things are funnier when they don’t make
Ironfist’s Grand Adventure
sense. Grandfather always said that the best jokes are the ones that make you think twice.”

They sat in comfortable silence for a while, listening to the crackle of the fire and the distanthooting of an owl. The mountain air was cold enough to see their breath, but the fire was warm,and Lily had snuggled into her sleeping bag with only her face poking out, like a caterpillar ina very colorful cocoon.
“Ironfist?” she said sleepily.
“Yes?”
“Do you think we’ll really find the Kingdom? The one in Grandfather’s stories?”
Ironfist stared into the flames, watching them dance and leap and die and be reborn, endlessly,tirelessly.
“I don’t know,” he admitted. “But I think the journey is worth taking, regardless of what we findat the end.”
But Lily was already asleep, her breathing slow and steady, a faint smile on her lips.
Chapter 8
The Enchanted Door
I

nside the mountain cave, past columns of glittering quartz and curtains of crystalline stalac-tites that chimed softly when the wind passed through them, they found a door. Not justany door — this was a door that clearly did not want to be found. It was set deep into theliving rock, its surface carved with runes so old that even the mountain guide (who claimed tospeak fourteen languages, including three that were technically extinct) could not decipher them.The wood was dark and ancient, harder than iron, and bound with bands of tarnished silver. Andat its center, gleaming like a tiny golden eye, was the most intricate keyhole Ironfist had everseen.
“It’s locked,” said Lily, stating the obvious with the unshakeable confidence of a ten-year-old.
“Clearly,” Ironfist replied, pushing against the door with his shoulder. It didn’t budge a millime-ter. He might as well have been pushing against the mountain itself.

Then, from a crevice in the cave wall, there came a brilliant flash of silver light — so bright thatIronfist and Lily both threw their arms over their eyes. When they lowered them again, a tinyfairy was hovering before the door on wings of iridescent glass. She was no bigger than Ironfist’shand, and she wore a dress that appeared to be made from woven moonlight and morning dew.
She pulled out her Wand — long, thin, and shimmering like asliver of captured starlight — and carefully inserted it into the
Ironfist’s Grand Adventure
tiny keyhole. The Wand fit perfectly, its slender form slidinginto the narrow opening as if it had been made for exactly thispurpose.

*Click.* The lock turned. Ancient dust cascaded from the doorframe, and the runes carved into the wood began to glow with asoft blue light, pulsing gently like a heartbeat. Slowly, with a groan that seemed to come fromthe very bones of the mountain, the door swung open.

A small fluffy white cat sat by the door, watching the proceedings with the politely bemusedexpression that cats reserve for events they consider beneath their dignity but mildly interestingnonetheless. It blinked its luminous green eyes, yawned enormously, and said:
MEOWMEOW!
“I think it wants to come with us,” said Lily, scooping the cat up into her arms. The cat purredlike a small, furry engine and settled against her chest with an air of absolute proprietorship.
“Fine,” said Ironfist, stepping through the enchanted doorway into the light beyond. “But if itgets us into trouble, you’re explaining it to Mother.”
Chapter 9
The City Park
T

he enchanted door — as enchanted doors are wont to do — deposited them somewhereentirely unexpected. Instead of the treasure vault or dragon’s lair that Ironfist had beenbracing himself for, they found themselves standing in the middle of a sun-drenchedcity park, surrounded by neatly trimmed hedges, flowering rose bushes, and the distant soundof an ice cream van playing a tinny rendition of “Greensleeves.”
“Where are we?” Lily asked, blinking in the sudden brightness, still clutching the cat.
“I have absolutely no idea,” Ironfist admitted.

“Let’s play baseball after school!” shouted a group of childrenrunning across the green field with bats and gloves. They wereplaying on a wide, flat stretch of grass, taking turns batting,pitching, and chasing fly balls with the carefree abandon of kidswho have absolutely nothing to worry about except whether it’stheir turn at bat.

Behind them, a red double-decker bus rumbled down the cob-blestone street, its upper deck packed with tourists snapping photographs. An old red telephonebooth stood on the corner like a silent guardian from another era, its glass panels clouded withage.
Ironfist’s Grand Adventure

Ironfist noticed something in the trees — a familiar pair of yellow eyes peering out from behind athick oak trunk. Another Goblin! But this one seemed different from the savage creature he hadfought in the Whispering Woods. This one was smaller, rounder, almost comical in appearance,and it was watching the children play baseball with an expression of undisguised fascination. Itheld an apple in one green hand and was munching on it contentedly, juice dribbling down itschin.
“Look,” Ironfist whispered to Lily, nodding toward the tree. “Not all Goblins are bad, it seems.”
The Goblin caught Ironfist’s eye, froze for a moment, then gave a tiny, almost sheepish wavebefore disappearing behind the tree with a rustle of leaves.
Chapter 10
Coming Home
T

he enchanted door, having delivered them to the park, had one final trick up its sleeve.After they spent a pleasant afternoon watching the baseball game, feeding the ducksin the pond, and sharing fish and chips on a park bench (Ironfist would later maintainthat the chips were almost as good as his mother’s), they found the door again — standingincongruously between two park benches, invisible to everyone except them.
This time, when they stepped through, they were home.

Willowmere had never looked so beautiful. The sunset was painting the sky in shades of amber,rose, and violet, and the smoke from their cottage chimney curled up into the golden light likea question mark.

“I told you to be careful,” said Ironfist, grinning at Lily as they walked through the garden gate.The garden was exactly as they had left it — Mother’s roses blooming in defiant abundance,the old apple tree leaning tiredly against the stone wall, and the birdbath where the sparrowscongregated every evening to gossip about the day’s events.
“Did you enjoy the Bakery we visited?” asked Lily, swinging her arms happily. “The one withthe golden sign? I keep thinking about those pastries.”
“Of course!” Ironfist laughed, the sound echoing across the quiet village green. “Best pastries
Ironfist’s Grand Adventure
in the Kingdom.”

He paused, realizing what he had said. The Kingdom. Was it real? Had any of it been real?The Goblin in the woods, the Subway ride, the Christmas feast, the mountains, the enchanteddoor … or had it all been some extraordinary dream conjured by the morning mist and a boy’soveractive imagination?

They passed by the old train station at the edge of the village.Ironfist remembered taking the Subway at the start of his journey— it felt like a lifetime ago. The spot where children had built aSnowman was now bare, the snow melted away, leaving only amuddy circle on the grass and a slightly sad-looking carrot lyingin the dirt.

Inside the house, Grandmother was waiting in her rocking chairby the fireplace, knitting something enormous and shapeless from bright purple yarn (it occurredto Ironfist that she bore a striking resemblance to the old woman on the Subway — but surelythat was just coincidence. Surely.).

“Old Mr. Henderson kicked the bucket last winter,” she whispered sadly, setting down her knit-ting. “He was a kind man. Always brought me eggs from his chickens on Sunday mornings.The village won’t be the same without him.”
Ironfist hugged his grandmother, breathing in the familiar scent of lavender soap and pepperminttea. Then he walked to the window and looked out.

The Kingdom stretched before him — peaceful, golden, and full of promise. Fields of wheatrippled in the evening breeze like an ocean of liquid gold. The mountains rose in the distance,purple and majestic against the fading sky. And somewhere out there, beyond the horizon, ad-ventures were waiting.
“Tomorrow,” he whispered to himself. “Tomorrow will bring a new adventure.”
The cat — which had followed them all the way from the enchanted cave — jumped onto thewindowsill, settled itself into a comfortable loaf, and began to purr.
THE END
